architecture.summary: The TP-Transformer augments the standard Transformer encoder-decoder with Tensor-Product (TP) Multi-Head Attention (TPMHA). Each attention head generates query, key, value, and role vectors from the hidden state. The role vector explicitly encodes relational structure; values are bound to roles via Hadamard product (diagonal of the full tensor product), and these filler-role bindings are summed to form a tensor-product representation (TPR) of a structured constituent. This binding occurs *within* the attention mechanism before summation across heads, allowing the model to distinguish between hierarchical and nested relations that standard attention conflates. The decoder mirrors the encoder structure but with two TPMHA layers per cell. Information flows from input tokens through character embedding, encoder layers of TPMHA and feed-forward blocks, to encoder output, which is then attended by the decoder (also with TP-Attention) to generate output tokens autoregressively.
trained_components: TP-Multi-Head Attention mechanism (query, key, value, role projections; learned binding weights)

biblio.title: Enhancing the Transformer with Explicit Relational Encoding for Math Problem Solving

claims.statement: TP-Transformer sets a new state of the art on the Mathematics Dataset, achieving 81.92% accuracy on interpolation
TP-Transformer achieves 54.67% accuracy on extrapolation test set
Among 56 modules, TP-Transformer achieves over 95% accuracy on 18 modules

Multi-Head Attention layers capture nearly the full information content of attended cells, not merely a subspace; empirical reconstruction mean squared error of ~0.017 for TP-Transformer and ~0.009 for regular Transformer when reconstructing from single-head value vectors

Standard stacked attention suffers from the binding problem: multiple layers of linear recombination cannot disambiguate between structures like (a/b)/(c/d) and (a/d)/(c/b) because the attention weights alone do not preserve role-filler bindings. TP-Attention resolves this by binding values to role vectors before summation, breaking structural symmetry and enabling unambiguous hierarchical representation.

Learned role vectors in the arithmetic__mixed module cluster into interpretable semantic roles including numerator and denominator positions; role assignments correctly swap when a fraction appears in the denominator of a fraction, simplifying division-of-fractions computation.

TP-Transformer attention maps show learned relations such as 'second-argument-of', where a digit cell queries for the operator of which it is the second argument, and can selectively attend across intervening numerals to maintain role consistency.

Hadamard product is used instead of full tensor product due to computational infeasibility; authors expect full tensor product would provide further improvement in performance and interpretability if compute resources permitted.
